text: This video demonstrates robot assisted surgery for bilateral pheochromocytoma in a fifteen year old girl.

A fifteen year old girl presented with hypertension in the last two years. Currently her blood pressure was one ninety by hundred and ten millimeters of mercury. She complained of palpitations and sweating episodes.
Her body mass index was twenty six and she gives a significant family history of younger brother who underwent surgery for left pheochromocytoma laparoscopically a month back by our team.

A CT scan revealed bilateral adrenal masses, the right one measuring approximately four and a half centimeters abutting the liver and the left one measuring approximately two point five centimeter directly relating to the left renal vein. There was an accessory inferior hepatic vein which was traveling very close to the adrenal mass.
Considering that this was a bilateral procedure, we had to plan the ports in such a way that most of the ports could be utilized for both sides and one extra working port was taken on each side.
Talking of the robot was over the ipsilateral shoulder with the patient in lateral position.

There are several challenges while preparing patients for pheochromocytoma surgery. We took a couple of weeks to stabilize the patient's blood pressure using alpha blockade followed by beta blockade and adequate fluid loading.

There were some challenges during the surgery with the fact that this was bilateral. There could be abandonment of the procedure after one side. There is always a risk of hemodynamic instability during the procedure. Proximity to major vascular structures makes it more challenging.
We also had to be careful that we do not render the patient adrenal insufficient and we had to do only partial adrenalectomy on the left side.

The surgeon begins the procedure on the right side by dissecting the tumor from the undersurface of the liver up to the diaphragm. Once the liver is adequately separated, attention is directed to the medial aspect of the tumor, where a plane is created between the inferior vena cava and the tumor.

The robotic instruments, utilizing endo-wrist movement, are used to precisely dissect the adrenal vein. The vein is clipped with hemolog clips and divided, while bedside assistance ensures safety near major vascular structures.
There is a chance of precipitous fall in blood pressure following this step. Hence communication with the anesthetist is very important.
The surgeon dissects the undersurface of the tumor and the upper pole of the kidney, carefully navigating around major vascular attachments.

The surgeon clips and divides vessels to prevent reactionary bleeding, taking care to preserve the vascular supply to the right kidney. During dissection, caution is exercised to avoid breaching the tumor capsule, with liberal use of clips and bipolar energy.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the bilateral adrenal tumor, aiming to preserve as much of the normal adrenal tissue as possible.

The surgeon identifies a second main adrenal vein draining into the inferior vena cava postrolaterally. The vascular nature of the pheochromocytoma is managed by securing its vascular attachments with hemlock clips and bipolar diathermy.
The surgeon has preserved the normal adrenal tissue to the maximum extent possible. The specimen is packed, and adequate hemostasis is visually confirmed.
Surgicel is placed to control low pressure, low volume bleeding. The specimen is positioned under the left lobe of the liver for easy retrieval during the operation on the opposite side.
The robotic surgical system is repositioned to facilitate surgery on the left side, ensuring optimal access and visualization for the procedure.

The surgeon dissects the left adrenal gland by separating surrounding structures, including the spleen, tail of the pancreas, upper pole of the kidney, and colon, without directly handling the adrenal itself.
The surgeon loops and takes control of the adrenal vein, as part of the adrenal sparing surgery, ensuring the vein is preserved.
The surgeon dissects the tumor-bearing lateral limb of the left adrenal gland and performs enucleoresection to remove the tumor from the adrenal gland.
The surgeon employs a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques, occasionally using hemolog clips to control bleeding, which is expected during the procedure.
The preserved left adrenal gland is visible, with hemostasis achieved. A surgical drain is placed, and the specimen, including the right-sided adrenal gland, is packed and retrieved.
The total operative time was hundred and eighty minutes with console time of one thirty six minutes. Blood loss was approximately one fifty ml.

The specimen is displayed, showing the right-sided adrenal gland with the tumor and the left-sided adrenal tumor separately. The histopathology results indicate bilateral pheochromocytoma with a pass score of three, suggesting benign disease.
Whole exome analysis revealed VHL positivity. The patient was kept in the ICU for overnight observation and shifted back to her room on post-operative day one.
She was discharged on post- drain removal. Blood pressure at the time of discharge was one ten by seventy millimeters of mercury with a pulse rate of eighty per minute.
Since this is a familial form of pheochromocytoma, genetic testing has been done. The plan is to keep on long-term follow-up for any possible recurrence or associated tumors.
In conclusion, bilateral procedure for Pheo in the same sitting is possible provided the patient is optimally prepared and remains hemodynamically stable. Preoperative preparation is very important.

Listed movements of the robotic instrument makes accurate movements possible during surgery and adrenal sparing surgery is necessary for bilateral cases and familial syndromes to prevent adrenal insufficiency. Thank you.
